overview:
In CS106A, loops are the backbone of repetition: they let your program perform the same action many times without duplicating code. You will learn when to choose a for, while, or do-while loop; how to control iterations with counters and conditions; and how to process collections using the enhanced for loop. Loops power tasks like summing values, validating input, drawing graphics repeatedly (in ACM libraries), and traversing arrays or strings.

We distinguish between definite loops (a known number of iterations) and indefinite loops (repeat until a condition or sentinel appears). You will practice structuring loop state with counters, accumulators, and flags; avoiding off-by-one and infinite loops; and reasoning about nested loops for 2D patterns. Mastery includes understanding loop invariants, variable scope within loop headers and bodies, and the proper use of break and continue.

teachingMaterials.keyConceptsSummary:
A loop repeatedly executes a block while a condition holds (while), for a fixed progression (for), or at least once before checking (do-while). Use for when you know the iteration count or have a simple counter pattern; use while for open-ended repetition controlled by a condition or sentinel; use do-while for at-least-once input validation or menus. The enhanced for loop (for-each) iterates through arrays/collections without explicit indexing.

Core mechanics: a loop has initialization (set up counters/state), a condition (tested before or after each iteration), a body (work per iteration), and an update (change state to move toward termination). Typical variables include a counter (tracks iterations), an accumulator (aggregates results), and sometimes a sentinel (special value to end input). Avoid off-by-one errors by being precise about inclusive/exclusive bounds and indices (0..n-1 for arrays). Prevent infinite loops by ensuring the condition eventually becomes false. Nested loops multiply work across two dimensions (rows/columns) and require careful control of inner vs. outer indices. Use break to exit early, continue to skip to the next iteration, and be mindful of scope: loop variables declared in headers are not visible outside.

Reasoning tools: state diagrams and loop invariants help you prove correctness—identify what must remain true before and after each iteration. Prefer clear loop conditions over cleverness, and test edge cases (empty input, single element, n=0) to catch boundary bugs.
learningObjectives: Choose the appropriate loop construct (for, while, do-while, enhanced for) for a given problem.
Write counter-controlled and sentinel-controlled loops that terminate correctly.
Use accumulators, counters, and flags to maintain loop state and compute aggregates.
Trace and debug loops, identifying off-by-one and infinite loop errors.
Design and analyze nested loops to generate patterns or traverse 2D data.
commonMistakes: Using <= instead of < (or vice versa) and indexing past array bounds.
Forgetting to update the loop variable or state, causing infinite loops.
Modifying a collection while using enhanced for, leading to ConcurrentModificationException (when using Java collections).
tipsForSuccess: Write the loop header last: first write the goal as comments, define what changes each iteration, then encode init/condition/update.
Test with edge cases (n=0, n=1, smallest/ largest inputs) and print intermediate values while debugging.
Keep loop bodies small; extract helper methods for clarity and easier reasoning about invariants.

vocabulary.definition: A definite loop with initialization, condition, and update in one header; ideal for counter-controlled repetition.
An indefinite loop that repeats while a boolean condition remains true; condition checked before each iteration.
A loop that executes the body at least once, then repeats while the condition remains true.
vocabulary.example: do { input = readInt("Enter (0 to quit): "); } while (input != 0);

vocabulary.definition: Also called for-each; iterates over elements of arrays/collections without explicit indices.
One execution of the loop body.
A variable that tracks the current iteration number or position.
A variable that aggregates results across iterations (sum, product, string build).
vocabulary.example: int sum = 0; for (int x : arr) { sum += x; }

vocabulary.definition: A special value signaling the end of input or termination of a loop.
The boolean expression controlling whether the loop continues.
The block of statements executed each iteration.
A loop inside another loop, often for 2D traversal or pattern generation.
A boundary mistake causing one too few or too many iterations.
vocabulary.example: for (int i = 0; i <= arr.length; i++) { /* i == arr.length crashes */ }

vocabulary.definition: A loop that never terminates because the condition never becomes false or there is no exit.
Immediately exits the nearest enclosing loop or switch.
Skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next condition check.

quiz.question: Which loop construct guarantees the body runs at least once?
quiz.explanation: do-while checks its condition after the first iteration, so the body executes at least once.

quiz.question: What is printed? int sum = 0; for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) { sum += i; } System.out.println(sum);
Which option correctly prints each element of int[] arr = {2,4,6};?
options: B) for (int i = 1; i <= arr.length; i++) { System.out.println(arr[i]); }
C) for (int i = 0; i <= arr.length; i++) { System.out.println(arr[i]); }
D) for (int x in arr) { System.out.println(x); }
quiz.explanation: Enhanced for iterates elements directly. B/C use invalid or out-of-bounds indexing; D is not Java syntax.

quiz.question: Which header runs exactly 10 times and visits i values 0..9?
quiz.explanation: Starting at 0 with condition i < 10 yields 10 iterations: 0 through 9.

quiz.question: Given int i = 0;, which loop is infinite?
quiz.explanation: for(;;) has no condition or updates and runs forever unless a break/return occurs.

quiz.question: What does continue do inside a loop?
options: B) Skips the rest of the current iteration and checks the loop condition
D) Repeats the current iteration without rechecking the condition
quiz.explanation: continue skips remaining body statements and proceeds to the next iteration's condition/update.

quiz.question: What is printed? int count = 0; for (int i=0; i<5; i++) { if (i == 2) continue; if (i == 4) break; count++; } System.out.println(count);
quiz.explanation: Increments when i=0,1,3 (skips 2, breaks at 4), so count is 3.

quiz.question: How many asterisks are printed? for (int r=0; r<3; r++) { for (int c=0; c<4; c++) { System.out.print("*"); } }
Which is the correct Java syntax for a do-while loop?
quiz.explanation: Java requires do { ... } while (condition); with a semicolon after the condition.

quiz.question: What happens? for (int i=0; i<3; i++) { } System.out.println(i);
options: C) Compile-time error: i cannot be resolved
quiz.explanation: i is scoped to the for loop header/body and not visible after the loop.

pseudocodeExamples.title: Sum of first N integers (definite loop)
pseudocodeExamples.description: Uses a counter-controlled for loop to accumulate a sum from 1 to N.
pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
set sum to 0
for i from 1 to N inclusive
  add i to sum
print sum

pseudocodeExamples.title: Sentinel-controlled average until -1
pseudocodeExamples.description: Reads integers until a sentinel value (-1) appears, then prints the average of the entered numbers.
pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
initialize total to 0 and count to 0
repeat
  read x
  if x is not -1
    add x to total
    increment count
until x is -1
if count > 0 then print total / count else print no data

pseudocodeExamples.title: Multiplication table with nested loops
pseudocodeExamples.description: Generates an n by n multiplication table using nested loops.
pseudocodeExamples.pseudocode:
for row from 1 to n
  for col from 1 to n
    compute product = row * col
    print product with spacing
  end inner
  print newline

syntaxReference: for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { /* body */ }

break; // exits nearest loop
continue; // skips to next iteration
commonPatterns: Counter-controlled loop: int count = 0; for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { count++; }
Accumulate sum/average: int sum = 0; for (int x : arr) { sum += x; } double avg = (arr.length>0)? (double)sum/arr.length : 0.0;
Find max/min: int max = Integer.MIN_VALUE; for (int x : arr) { if (x > max) max = x; }
Sentinel input: while ((x = scan.nextInt()) != -1) { /* process x */ }
Nested loops grid: for (int r=0;r<rows;r++){ for(int c=0;c<cols;c++){ System.out.print("*"); } System.out.println(); }
quickTips: Prefer i < array.length over i <= array.length to avoid IndexOutOfBoundsException.
Ensure each loop makes progress toward termination (update counters or state every iteration).
Keep loop bodies small; extract helper methods for clarity and testability.
